turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion for the Court in 23-1324, Perttu versus Richards. The Prison Litigation Reform Act or the PLRA requires prisoners with complaints about prison conditions to raise those complaints in a prison grievance process before bringing suit in federal court. That is called exhausting the process. Now in some cases, a dispute will arise over whether a prisoner has complied with this exhaustion requirement. This case involves Kyle Richards, a prisoner in Michigan. He alleges that he was sexually abused by Thomas Perttu, a prison employee. He also alleges that when he tried to file the required grievance forms about the abuse, Perttu destroyed the forms and threatened to kill him if he filed more. Richards sued Perttu for violating his constitutional rights including his First Amendment right to file grievances. Perttu responded that Richards had failed to exhaust available grievance procedures as required by the PLRA. Richards' response was of course that is the whole point, destroying the grievance forms was a key part of the First Amendment violation in the first place. Now if Perttu did destroy Richards' grievance forms, Richards had done all he could do to exhaust the prison procedures and Perttu's preventing him from raising his claims violated the First Amendment's. If Perttu had not destroyed the grievance forms, he had not prevented Richards from raising any claims and Richards would lose. The procedural issue about exhaustion and the substantive issue about the First Amendment claim were, as the parties agree, intertwined, deciding one, would decide the other. A Magistrate Judge held a hearing on the case. The judge decided that Richards' witnesses were not credible and so ruled that Richards had failed to exhaust. Richards argues, however, that the judge should never have decided the question and that it should have been decided by a jury, that is because Richards is entitled to a jury on his First Amendment claim even if he may not be entitled to a jury on the PLRA exhaustion issue. So by deciding the exhaustion issue first, Richards says the judge effectively decided his First Amendment claim as well depriving him of his right to a jury Trial. The Court of Appeals agreed with Richards and reversed the Magistrate Judge, ruling that a jury should have been convened to decide the intertwined question. The Court recognize that it had circuit precedent saying that judges can ordinarily resolve exhaustion disputes without a jury, but when an exhaustion dispute is intertwined with the merits, the Court said, a jury must be called to resolve the intertwined issue. That decision of the Sixth Circuit conflicted with a contrary decision from the nearby Seventh Circuit and we granted review to resolve the split. We now affirm the question of what to do when a non-jury issue and a jury issue are effectively one and the same has come up before. For example, if you want to bring a case in federal court rather than state court, you have to show that your claim is worth more than today $75,000.00. Well, what if there is a question about the value of the property the parties are fighting over? Over a century ago, we confronted that question in the case involving a Mr. Smithers. Mr. Smithers said that a group of defendants had conspired together to each steal a different part of his land and that the total value of all that stolen land together was above the minimum for getting his case into federal court, but if the defendants had not conspired together, then Mr. Smithers could not add up the value of all the stolen land and so he would not meet the minimum for getting into federal court. So the question was, who should decide whether the defendants conspired together, a judge, or a jury? We held that it had to be a jury because the question of whether the defendants conspired together was intertwined with the merits of Mr. Smithers' case. Or consider another of our cases, Beacon Theatres, it is called. Two movie theaters were fighting over whether one of them had violated the antitrust laws. That theater sued for a declaratory judgment that it had not violated the antitrust laws. That kind of claim does not require a jury, but the other theater responded by suing for money damages, a kind of claim that does require a jury, and both of those claims depended on the common issue of whether there was an antitrust violation. So the question again, could the judge decide the issue or would it have to be a jury? And again, we held that it had to be a jury. In fact, when the PLRA was enacted, the usual practice of federal courts in a variety of context was to have a jury decide factual disputes that are intertwined with the merits. Nothing in the PLRA suggests that Congress intended to depart from that practice in situations like this one. For those reasons and others laid out in the Court's opinion, the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit is affirmed. Justice Barrett has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Thomas, Alito and Kavanaugh join.
